Curriculum → Networking, edge, and request routing → Content delivery networks

Lesson 2 of 5

Content delivery networks

A CDN moves cacheable content closer to users and can absorb large amounts of read traffic before requests ever reach origin infrastructure.

25-35 min Networking, edge, and request routing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

For global products, edge caching is often the fastest latency improvement and the cheapest way to reduce origin load.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. What belongs at the edge Use cache-control headers to express freshness2. Push vs. pull models Pre-warm popular content before launches3. Trade-offs and metrics Personalized responses lower cacheability

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing For global products, edge caching is often the fastest latency improvement and the cheapest way to reduce origin load. 2. What belongs at the edge Use cache-control headers to express freshness3. Push vs. pull models Pre-warm popular content before launches4. Trade-offs and metrics Personalized responses lower cacheability
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

For global products, edge caching is often the fastest latency improvement and the cheapest way to reduce origin load.

What to say first

  • Separate cacheable, immutable, and personalized responses.
  • Mention cache keys, TTLs, and invalidation.
  • Use the CDN as both performance and protection layer.
2

Study section

What belongs at the edge

Static assets, media, rendered fragments, and sometimes API responses with clear caching semantics are good CDN candidates.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use cache-control headers to express freshness
  • Separate immutable assets from personalized data
  • Origin shielding can reduce thundering herds toward the origin
3

Study section

Push vs. pull models

Push CDNs pre-load content; pull CDNs fetch on demand and cache after the first request. Most web products rely on pull behavior for simplicity.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Pre-warm popular content before launches
  • Think about invalidation for mutable assets
  • Signed URLs help protect private content
4

Study section

Trade-offs and metrics

Cache hit ratio, origin offload, egress cost, and regional latency tell you whether the CDN is doing real work.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Personalized responses lower cacheability
  • Hot content benefits from long TTLs and immutable naming
  • CDNs can also terminate TLS and apply bot/rate controls

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

Edge caches reduce user distance and shield the origin from repeated reads.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Separate cacheable, immutable, and personalized responses.
  • Mention cache keys, TTLs, and invalidation.
  • Use the CDN as both performance and protection layer.
  • Track hit ratio and origin offload.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Caching personalized responses without varying on identity or headers.
  • Ignoring invalidation for mutable assets.
  • Assuming a CDN removes the need for origin capacity planning.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What content would you intentionally avoid putting behind a CDN?
  • How would you roll out a new web bundle without stale asset bugs?
  • Why can a CDN lower both latency and cost?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

DNS fundamentals Networking, edge, and request routingCaching layers and cache placement Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Content delivery networks

Watch the edge, cache, and origin layers react as traffic shape changes across regions and protection controls. Apply it to content delivery networks so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "Content delivery networks" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around what belongs at the edge and watch how it interacts with push vs. pull models.For global products, edge caching is often the fastest latency improvement and the cheapest way to reduce origin load.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • CDN hit ratio
  • origin utilization
  • edge p95
  • policy saturation

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 120000 rps · 5 ms
  • Line 2: edge · 95000 rps · 8 ms

  • Line 4: service · 42000 rps · 10 ms
  • Line 5: service · 22000 rps · 18 ms
  • Line 6: service · 50000 rps · 6 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Separate cacheable, immutable, and personalized responses.
  • Mention cache keys, TTLs, and invalidation.
  • Use the CDN as both performance and protection layer.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from What belongs at the edge and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Separate cacheable, immutable, and personalized responses. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from Push vs. pull models.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "Content delivery networks" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • What content would you intentionally avoid putting behind a CDN?
  • What belongs at the edge
  • Push vs. pull models
  • Separate cacheable, immutable, and personalized responses.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
